input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A building as the physical asset within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, encompassing its entire lifecycle from design through construction, operation, and refurbishment phases. The system is characterized by the integration of static models with real-time operational data to enable simulation, analysis, and optimization for data-driven infrastructure management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Simulation, analysis, and optimization capabilities; real-time insights monitoring; predictive analytics for infrastructure health; and enhanced decision-making support targeting AECO stakeholders to reduce operational costs and improve collaboration across the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Multiple digital twin instances are managed through a centralized DT Framework coordinating roles including Building Control Engineers (device modeling), Facility Managers (real-time monitoring and control), and DT Engineers (service integration via standardized extension points), enabling hierarchical coordination for building lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system employs a modular architecture with a centralized hub facilitating bidirectional data exchange and coordinating roles such as Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers, using standardized extension points for integrating custom analytics and services to support holistic building lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability across heterogeneous systems is addressed as a non-functional requirement, with the modular architecture's standardized extension points enabling integration of custom services and analytics; specific data exchange protocols or dependencies on external systems are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin utilizes Building Information Modeling (BIM) for geometric representation, where Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators, controllers, and control logic within the BIM environment to establish a unified semantic foundation. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles transform enriched BIM models into structured representations, automatically generating deployable DT artifacts. Real-time data exchange synchronizes physical building operations with the digital counterpart, integrating sensor and actuator data for dynamic simulation and control.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The system evaluates the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric to measure how well the DT reflects its physical counterpart through continuous data synchronization and model validation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment utilizes a cloud-based model-driven framework for scalable DT instances, with Unreal Engine 5 providing high-fidelity visualization for immersive monitoring and control interfaces. The modular architecture includes standardized extension points for seamless integration of custom analytics and services without full redeployment, ensuring low-overhead updates and high availability.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses device modeling within BIM (Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators, and controllers to address BIM's static limitations), Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation to extract structured representations and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts, runtime operation with real-time data synchronization and interactive visualization for system monitoring and control, and evolution through modular integration of custom services (DT Engineers) to adapt to operational requirements, with Facility Managers overseeing monitoring and control activities.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process utilizes Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to transform enriched BIM models into structured representations and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is managed via a modular architecture featuring standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, enabling dynamic adaptation to changing operational needs.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is facilitated through immersive interactive visualization interfaces for real-time monitoring and control by Facility Managers, with analytics capabilities provided by custom services developed by DT Engineers to process real-time data and generate operational insights.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with BIM standards for semantic modeling of building components, standardized extension points for interoperable service integration, and a unified semantic foundation addressing BIM's limitations in representing Building Control System (BCS) components within AECO industry contexts.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the system as a building within the AECO domain and correctly details the lifecycle scope and the integration of static and real-time data. It is highly specific and grounded in the text. Quotes: 'virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building)', 'architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain', 'along the complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments'.

The extracted services (simulation, monitoring, optimization, decision support) align perfectly with the text's description of Digital Twin capabilities and their benefits to stakeholders. Quotes: 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities', 'reduced operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved stakeholder collaboration'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly lists specific tools and frameworks used for implementation, such as Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and Unreal Engine. Quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the source text's emphasis on real-time data exchange. The text explicitly states: 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and 'Real-time data integration... enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins'. It also mentions 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'. While specific frequencies or latency numbers are absent, the core concept of real-time, continuous synchronization is well-supported.

The description correctly identifies the roles and the centralized framework. The source provides a table defining 'Actor Roles' including Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers, and states the framework 'acts as a central hub'. However, the text does not explicitly describe managing 'multiple digital twin instances' of the same entity or complex hierarchies of instances; it focuses on a single deployment pipeline for a building. The extraction is specific to the roles but slightly overstates the multiplicity of instances compared to the single-instance focus of the source.

The description is highly detailed and strongly grounded in the source. The source describes a 'model-driven deployment framework' with a 'modular architecture' (R4) and 'standardized extension points'. It explicitly lists the coordination of roles: Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers. The text summarizes this orchestration by stating, 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management'.

The extraction correctly identifies interoperability as a non-functional requirement and notes the lack of specific protocol details. The source text lists 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' under non-functional requirements and mentions 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'. It generally cites 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' for connectivity but does not provide detailed data exchange protocols or external system dependencies. The extraction accurately reflects the level of detail provided.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately and comprehensively reflects the source text, detailing the use of BIM for geometry and semantics, the role of Building Control Engineers, the use of MDE for generating artifacts, and real-time data synchronization. Quotes: 'BIM enables the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models'; 'Building Control Engineer: ... define the control logic'; 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations'; 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization'.

The source text mentions actuators solely in the context of modeling and configuration (e.g., 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment') but does not provide the specific physical details, types, ranges, or safety constraints required by the characteristic description. Therefore, the extraction stating 'Not in Document' is correct.

Similar to actuators, the text mentions sensors only in the abstract context of modeling and runtime semantics (e.g., 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers') and provides no specific details on sensor types, distribution, or technical specifications. Thus, the extraction 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction correctly identifies the ODTE metric used to evaluate the DT's reflection of its physical counterpart. Quotes supporting this: 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' and 'measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric'.

Accurately describes the Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) approach and the modular architecture for evolution. It aligns perfectly with the proposed solution's methodology and extensibility features. Supported by quotes: 'model-driven deployment framework', 'automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention', and 'standardized extension points for modular service integration'.

It links these capabilities to the correct user roles. Supported by quotes: 'interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control' and 'supports the integration of custom services and analytics'.

The description accurately reflects the use of BIM as a semantic foundation and standard, the need for standardized extension points for interoperability, and the specific issue of BIM's limitations regarding Building Control Systems (BCS) components.

standardized extension points for modular service integration

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

While the text mentions 'governance' in the context of MDE encoding organizational policies, it does not specifically address data ownership or privacy. Therefore, the extraction 'Not in Document' is correct.

The source text discusses 'robust connectivity and standard compliance' in the context of related frameworks like Eclipse Ditto and addresses 'integrity' regarding semantic drift. However, it does not describe specific cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, or fail-safe mechanisms for the Digital Twin. Thus, the extraction 'Not in Document' is accurate.
